questions.question_text: What is the most common foreclosure method in Arizona?
questions.explanation: Non-judicial foreclosure (trustee's sale) is the most common method in Arizona because deeds of trust include a power of sale clause. It is faster and less expensive than judicial foreclosure.

questions.question_text: What is the minimum waiting period between recording the Notice of Trustee's Sale and the actual sale?
questions.explanation: After the trustee records the Notice of Trustee's Sale, there is a minimum 91-day waiting period before the public auction. This gives the borrower time to cure the default or arrange alternatives.

questions.question_text: After a trustee's sale in Arizona, does the borrower have a right of redemption?
questions.correct_answer: No, there is no redemption after a trustee's sale
distractors: Yes, for 6 months after the trustee's sale
Yes, for 30 days after the trustee's sale
Only if the property was a primary residence
questions.explanation: Unlike judicial foreclosure, there is NO right of redemption after a non-judicial trustee's sale in Arizona. This makes the sale final and gives the buyer clear title immediately.

questions.question_text: At a trustee's sale, the property goes to:
questions.correct_answer: The highest bidder at public auction
questions.explanation: The trustee's sale is a public auction where the property is sold to the highest bidder. The lender may submit a credit bid up to the amount owed, but any third party can outbid them.

questions.question_text: A borrower receives a Notice of Trustee's Sale. They want to stop the foreclosure. What is their best option within the 91-day period?
questions.correct_answer: Cure the default by paying the arrears
questions.explanation: During the 91-day waiting period, the borrower can cure the default by catching up on missed payments (reinstatement). There is no post-sale redemption right in a trustee's sale, so acting before the sale is critical.

questions.question_text: How long is the right of redemption after a judicial foreclosure sale in Arizona?
questions.correct_answer: 6 months, or 30 days if the property was abandoned
distractors: 12 months for all property types regardless
90 days for residential property after the sale
No redemption right exists in any type of foreclosure
questions.explanation: After judicial foreclosure, the borrower has a 6-month right of redemption (or 30 days if the property was abandoned). This contrasts with non-judicial foreclosure, which has no redemption right.

distractors: Only a Notice of Trustee's Sale
Agreement from both borrower and lender
questions.explanation: Judicial foreclosure requires the lender to file a lawsuit and obtain a court judgment. This is more time-consuming and expensive than non-judicial foreclosure, which is why it is less commonly used.

questions.question_text: After a trustee's sale of a commercial property, how long does the lender have to file a deficiency action?
distractors: No deficiency is ever allowed after a trustee's sale
questions.explanation: ARS §33-814(A) allows a deficiency action within 90 days after a trustee's sale when the property does not qualify for §33-814(G) protection (a one- or two-family dwelling on 2.5 acres or less). Both foreclosure methods permit deficiency judgments, each subject to its own anti-deficiency statute.

questions.question_text: Why do most Arizona lenders prefer non-judicial foreclosure over judicial foreclosure?
questions.correct_answer: It is faster and less expensive
distractors: It provides a longer redemption period
questions.explanation: Non-judicial foreclosure is preferred because it avoids court proceedings, making it faster and less costly. For a one- or two-family dwelling on 2.5 acres or less, ARS §33-814(G) bars any deficiency after a trustee's sale; for other property, the lender may still sue for a deficiency within 90 days under §33-814(A).

questions.question_text: An abandoned property is sold through judicial foreclosure. The borrower wants to redeem it. How long do they have?
distractors: No redemption right for abandoned property
questions.explanation: For abandoned properties sold through judicial foreclosure, the redemption period is shortened to 30 days (versus the standard 6 months). The property must be genuinely abandoned for this shorter period to apply.

questions.question_text: Arizona's anti-deficiency statute (ARS §33-814(G)) protects borrowers on which type of loans?
questions.correct_answer: Any deed-of-trust loan on a property of 2.5 acres or less used as a one- or two-family dwelling
distractors: All residential mortgage loans regardless of loan type
Commercial property loans under $500,000 in value
questions.explanation: ARS §33-814(G) bars a deficiency after a trustee's sale based on the property — 2.5 acres or less, limited to and utilized for a single one-family or single two-family dwelling — not on the loan's purpose. The purchase-money limitation belongs to judicial foreclosure under ARS §33-729(A).

questions.question_text: A homeowner refinances their original purchase-money loan on a 1-acre primary residence. After a trustee's sale, can the lender pursue a deficiency?
questions.correct_answer: No, the anti-deficiency protection still applies
distractors: Yes, refinanced loans lose anti-deficiency protection
Only if the deficiency amount exceeds $50,000
Only if the home is worth less than $250,000
questions.explanation: ARS §33-814(G) bars a deficiency after a trustee's sale of a qualifying one- or two-family dwelling on 2.5 acres or less regardless of whether the loan was refinanced. Even in judicial foreclosure, Helvetica Servicing v. Pasquan held that a straight refinance retains its purchase-money character; only cash-out portions can be traced and recovered.

questions.question_text: A borrower purchased a 5-acre residential property with a purchase-money loan. Is the borrower protected by the anti-deficiency statute?
questions.correct_answer: No, the property exceeds the 2.5-acre limit
distractors: Yes, all purchase-money loans are protected
Yes, as long as it is a primary residence
Only if the loan is under $500,000
questions.explanation: The anti-deficiency statute only applies to residential property of 2.5 acres or less with a one-family or two-family dwelling. At 5 acres, this property exceeds the size limit and is not protected.

questions.question_text: In a short sale, the property is sold for:
questions.correct_answer: Less than the mortgage balance with lender approval
distractors: More than the current appraised market value
Exactly the outstanding loan payoff amount
Whatever the seller independently decides to accept
questions.explanation: A short sale occurs when the property is sold for less than the outstanding mortgage balance, requiring lender approval. The lender agrees to accept the reduced payoff rather than proceeding with foreclosure.

questions.question_text: A borrower voluntarily transfers title to the lender to avoid foreclosure. This is called:
questions.explanation: A deed in lieu of foreclosure is a voluntary transfer of title from the borrower to the lender. It avoids the time and expense of foreclosure for both parties but may still have tax consequences for forgiven debt.
